steps.title: Map what Richard actually does in the passage
steps.content: Richard openly confesses two things: (1) he is the one who caused Clarence's arrest ("whom I indeed have cast in darkness") and (2) he weeps to Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham while blaming it on the queen ("I do beweep to many simple"; "tell them 'tis the Queen and her allies"). The correct answer must capture both his false public grief and his secret true responsibility.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Richard attributes Clarence's troubles to both his own secret plotting and the distrust...the queen and her allies...have planted': misreads the passage — Richard says the queen planted nothing; he invented that story himself to deceive Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Richard describes having wept...and says that the earnestness of his grief caused them to accept his version': partially accurate but omits the key fact that Richard himself caused Clarence's arrest, making the grief false rather than merely earnest. The choice frames the deception as incomplete. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Richard acknowledges that his mischievous nature has spurred him...but he reports that he has hitherto not lost the trust of the queen and her allies': Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham are not the queen's allies — Richard tells them the queen is to blame in order to win their trust and turn them against her. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Richard indicates that he has pretended to be aggrieved...and has proclaimed it to be the fault of the queen and her allies, but in reality, he has caused the hostility': accurately captures all three elements — the false display of grief, the false blame placed on the queen, and Richard's actual responsibility for Clarence's situation. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
